food_name: Har Gau (steamed shrimp dumpling, 4 pieces = 1 basket, ~60g)
aliases: उबले हुए झींगा पकौड़ी (Ubal Hue Jhinga Pakodi)

hindi: उबले हुए झींगा पकौड़ी (Ubal Hue Jhinga Pakodi)

region_of_origin: Guangdong (Canton), southern China; widely adopted in Singapore, Hong Kong, Malaysia, and wherever Cantonese dim sum is served. Har gau is considered the benchmark test of a dim sum chef's skill in Cantonese culinary tradition.
food_subgroup: Steamed dumpling — shrimp/prawn filling in wheat-starch and tapioca-starch translucent wrapper. Standard serve: 4 pieces per bamboo steamer basket = ~60g. Each piece ~15g.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific Cantonese har gau class. FOODON_03315091 (steamed dumpling) is the closest defensible parent. A species-specific shrimp-filled dim sum class does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed dumpling — FoodEx2 composite food category for steamed filled dumplings; used as closest-parent anchor in the absence of a Cantonese dim sum-specific node.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a har gau-specific code. A0FKP (steamed dumpling, composite) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Documented as closest_parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for har gau or Cantonese steamed shrimp dumpling exists in the current release.

allergens: gluten (wheat starch — trace; tapioca starch is gluten-free but cross-contamination risk exists)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 1 bamboo steamer basket containing 4 pieces of har gau, weighing approximately 60g in total (each piece ~15g).

nutrient_source.title: Har Gau (steamed shrimp dumpling) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for har gau (steamed shrimp dumpling). Values are reported per 100g edible portion. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy. The foodo.sg portal is the public-facing interface for the same HPB dataset.

_per_100g_missing_data_notes.monounsaturated_fat_g: Not reported in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this item. Value set to -1 per Veritas numeric hygiene convention for unreported nutrients.
_per_100g_missing_data_notes.polyunsaturated_fat_g: Not reported in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this item. Value set to -1 per Veritas numeric hygiene convention for unreported nutrients.
_per_100g_missing_data_notes.water_g: Not reported in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this item. Value set to -1 per Veritas numeric hygiene convention for unreported nutrients.
_per_100g_missing_data_notes.trans_fat_g: Reported as 0.0 — confirmed trace/negligible in HPB source for this steamed preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat starch-based steamed dumplings — University of Sydney GI Database and supporting peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for har gau exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~48 (low) is an analogous estimate based on: (1) wheat starch (the primary wrapper ingredient) has a substantially lower GI than regular wheat flour because the gluten protein is removed during processing, leaving nearly pure starch granules that gelatinise differently; (2) University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat starch-based foods cluster in the GI 40–55 range; (3) the steaming preparation method does not raise GI relative to baking or frying. GL per 60g serving = (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (48 × 11.4) / 100 ≈ 5.5. This estimate is consistent with published dim sum nutritional analyses and is documented as analogous_estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 367 mg/100g; cumulative sodium risk at yum cha
hypertension.guidance: One 60g basket of har gau contributes approximately 220 mg sodium, which is acceptable in isolation; however, har gau is almost always consumed with soy dipping sauce (1 teaspoon ≈ 350 mg sodium), effectively doubling the sodium load per basket, and a typical yum cha meal of 8–10 dishes with condiments can deliver 2,000–3,000 mg sodium — well above the 2,000 mg/day target for hypertensive individuals. Patients with hypertension should limit soy sauce use and restrict total dim sum dish count per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~48 (low), GL ~5.5 per 60g basket, high-quality shrimp protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Har gau is among the most favourable dim sum choices for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: the wheat-starch wrapper has a low GI (~48) compared with regular wheat flour products, the glycemic load per basket is low (~5.5), and the high shrimp protein content (12g/100g) moderates postprandial glucose response; the primary clinical concern at yum cha is cumulative carbohydrate from multiple high-GI dishes (e.g., lo mai gai, cheung fun with sweet sauce) rather than har gau itself.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8g/100g); steamed preparation; shrimp cholesterol 85mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Har gau is steamed (not fried), contains low saturated fat (0.8g/100g, ~0.5g per basket), and provides heart-healthy shrimp protein; dietary cholesterol from shrimp (85mg/100g) has minimal impact on LDL in most individuals per current evidence, but patients on strict dietary cholesterol restriction should note the contribution; the cardiovascular risk at yum cha is primarily driven by cumulative sodium from multiple dishes and condiments rather than har gau's intrinsic fat profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Har gau (蝦餃 / 虾饺, Cantonese: Haa1 Gaau2) is the gold-standard benchmark dish of Cantonese dim sum and yum cha culture, originating in Guangdong, southern China, and now ubiquitous in Singapore, Hong Kong, Malaysia, and Chinese diaspora restaurants worldwide. The dish consists of whole fresh shrimp or prawn filling — typically seasoned with bamboo shoots, ginger, sesame oil, and a touch of lard — encased in a thin, pleated, translucent wrapper made from wheat starch and tapioca starch. The translucency of the wrapper, through which the pink shrimp filling is visible, and the precise pleating (traditionally 7–10 folds) are the hallmarks of a skilled dim sum chef. Har gau is always steamed in bamboo steamers; a standard serve is 4 pieces per basket (~60g). Texturally, the wrapper is silky, slightly chewy, and delicate; the filling is firm, juicy, and sweet with a clean prawn flavour. Nutritionally, har gau is one of the most favourable dim sum items: approximately 85 kcal per 60g basket, 7.2g protein, 1.6g fat, 11.4g carbohydrate, GI ~48 (low), GL ~5.5. Per 100g: 142 kcal, 12g protein, 2.7g fat (0.8g saturated), 19g carbohydrate, 1g fibre, 367mg sodium. The wheat-starch wrapper has a substantially lower GI than regular wheat flour products because gluten removal alters starch gelatinisation. Clinically, har gau is recommended for Type 2 Diabetes patients at yum cha due to its low GI, low GL, and high protein content. The primary clinical concern is sodium: soy dipping sauce adds ~350mg sodium per teaspoon, doubling the per-basket sodium load. Har gau is dairy-free, not halal-certified by default (lard may be used), and contains crustacean allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. In Singapore, har gau is a staple of weekend yum cha at Chinese restaurants and hotel buffets and is commonly ordered alongside siu mai, cheung fun, and lo mai gai.
common_user_queries: har gau vs siu mai nutrition
how many calories in 4 pieces har gau
meal_context: Singapore hawker centre (less common — primarily restaurant dish)

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (primary approved source for Singapore dishes). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat-starch food cluster, documented as analogous_estimate. All values are internally consistent and cross-checked against published dim sum nutritional literature.
notes: Har gau (蝦餃) is widely regarded as the most technically demanding dish in the Cantonese dim sum repertoire. The wrapper is made from a blend of wheat starch (澄粉, chéng fěn) and tapioca starch (生粉), which produces the characteristic translucency and silky texture. Traditional recipes include a small amount of lard in the filling for flavour and mouthfeel; modern health-conscious versions may substitute vegetable oil, which affects the halal status. The standard serve in Singapore restaurants is 4 pieces per bamboo steamer basket (~60g); some establishments serve 3 pieces (~45g). The input record contained a serving description inconsistency (3 pieces = 75g vs. 4 pieces = 60g); this output standardises to 4 pieces = 60g as the canonical Singapore restaurant serve consistent with HPB source data. Missing-data fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) are set to -1 because the HPB source does not report these values for this entry — they are not zero. The GI of ~48 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for har gau does not appear in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date.
